content.text: Environment Promotion Gates and Approvals

Environment promotion is the controlled process of moving a Claude feature from one environment to the next — dev to staging, staging to canary, canary to production. Each transition has an explicit gate: a set of automated checks and, for later stages, human approvals. Without promotion gates, features advance on hope rather than evidence.

attrs.alt: Promotion pipeline: Dev → CI Gates → Staging → Staging Gates + PM sign-off → Canary (5%) → Canary Health Check → Production (100%)

content.text: The Promotion Pipeline
Why Claude applications need explicit promotion gates
Traditional application promotion gates focus on code correctness and infrastructure health. Claude applications add three dimensions that standard gates do not cover:
Prompt behaviour:
the prompt template version deployed to production must match what was evaluated in staging. A mismatch — even a single character — can produce different model behaviour.
Model version:
the pinned model ID must be consistent across all environments. A template evaluated against one model version should not be promoted to an environment running a different version.
Eval pass rate continuity:

the pass rate measured in staging is the baseline for production monitoring. If the production pass rate deviates significantly, the difference must be explained before concluding the feature is healthy.
Gate 1 — Dev to Staging Promotion
Prerequisites
A feature is eligible for staging deployment only when all CI gates on the main branch are green. The release pipeline checks these automatically and blocks the staging deployment if any are red:
Check
Source
Block or warn
All CI gates green (static, unit, prompt)
GitHub Actions status on main
Block
Prompt template version in code matches registry
Registry consistency check
Block
No secrets detected in diff
detect-secrets result
Block
DoD issue linked to the deployment
Release automation checks PR body for DoD issue link
Warn (blocking for major versions)

# .github/workflows/promote-to-staging.yml
name: Promote to Staging

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  pre-promote-checks:
    name: Pre-Promotion Checks
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Verify all CI gates passed on main
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const checks = await github.rest.checks.listForRef({
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              ref: context.sha,
            });
            const required = ['static-analysis', 'unit-tests', 'prompt-gates'];
            const failed = checks.data.check_runs
              .filter(c => required.includes(c.name) && c.conclusion !== 'success');
            if (failed.length > 0) {
              core.setFailed(`CI gates not all green: ${failed.map(c => c.name).join(', ')}`);
            }

      - name: Check template registry consistency
        run: python3 scripts/check-registry-consistency.py

  deploy-staging:
    name: Deploy to Staging
    needs: pre-promote-checks
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: staging
    steps:
      - name: Deploy application to staging
        run: ./scripts/deploy.sh staging ${{ github.sha }}

      - name: Run post-deploy smoke test
        run: python3 scripts/smoke-test.py --environment staging --timeout 60
Gate 2 — Staging to Canary Promotion
Staging gate checks
Staging promotion requires both automated gate passage and human approval. The automated checks run first; human approval is requested only after all automated checks pass:

# .github/workflows/promote-to-canary.yml
name: Promote to Canary

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      staging_sha:
        description: 'The SHA deployed to staging to promote'
        required: true

jobs:
  staging-gates:
    name: Staging Automated Gates
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: staging
    steps:
      - name: Run full eval suite in staging
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.STAGING_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          python3 scripts/run-staging-eval.py             --sha ${{ github.event.inputs.staging_sha }}             --output staging-eval-results.json

      - name: Check eval gates
        run: python3 scripts/check-staging-gates.py staging-eval-results.json

      - name: Run load test (100 RPS for 5 minutes)
        run: k6 run scripts/load-test.js --env ENV=staging

      - name: Upload gate evidence
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: staging-gate-evidence-${{ github.run_id }}
          path: staging-eval-results.json

  human-approval:
    name: PM Approval Required
    needs: staging-gates
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment:
      name: pre-production
      # GitHub environment protection requires a named approver to proceed
    steps:
      - name: Deployment approved — promote to canary
        run: echo "Canary promotion approved by ${{ github.actor }}"
Who approves staging-to-canary
The approval requirement depends on the change tier:
Change tier
Required approver(s)
SLA for approval
Fallback
Patch (metadata only)
Any team member
1 business day
Auto-approve after 24h if no objection
Minor (wording, no capability change)
PM or product owner
1 business day
Escalate to EM if PM unavailable after 1 day
Minor (capability scope change)
PM + prompt lead
1 business day
Cannot ship without both; escalate to EM after 2 days
Major (safety baseline change)
PM + prompt lead + security reviewer
2 business days
Cannot ship without all three; no auto-escalation
Gate 3 — Canary Deployment
Canary configuration
Canary runs at 5% of production traffic for a minimum of 30 minutes. The feature is automatically aborted and traffic reverted to the previous version if any of the canary abort conditions fire:

# release/canary-config.yml
canary:
  traffic_split: 0.05        # 5% of production traffic
  soak_minutes: 30           # minimum time before full promotion
  auto_abort_on:
    error_rate_threshold: 0.03        # abort if > 3% errors (vs baseline < 1%)
    latency_p95_threshold_ms: 5000    # abort if P95 > 5s (vs SLA of 3s)
    cost_spike_multiplier: 2.0        # abort if cost/req > 2× staging baseline
    rollback_signal: true             # abort if on-call triggers manual rollback
  post_soak_check:
    eval_sample_size: 50    # run 50 live requests through the eval judge
    min_pass_rate: 0.88     # must pass at ≥ 88% to proceed to full rollout
Canary monitoring during soak
During the 30-minute soak, the release engineer (or automated pipeline) monitors these signals in real time:
Error rate:
canary vs baseline in side-by-side Grafana panel. A > 2× difference at any point during the soak is an abort signal.
P95 latency:
canary model latency vs baseline. The canary may show higher latency if the new prompt version is longer; this is expected. Latency > SLA is an abort signal.
stop_reason=max_tokens rate:
if the new prompt generates longer responses, max_tokens truncation may increase. > 5% rate is an alert (not auto-abort).
cost per request:
new prompt versions with longer system prompts cost more per call. > 2× baseline is an auto-abort signal.
Gate 4 — Canary to Full Production
Post-soak evaluation
After 30 minutes of canary traffic, the pipeline runs a final automated evaluation before promoting to 100%:

# scripts/post-soak-eval.py
import asyncio, json, random
import anthropic

async def sample_live_eval(environment: str, sample_size: int, golden: list) -> dict:
    """
    Run a sample of golden cases against the live canary endpoint
    to verify real production conditions match staging eval results.
    """
    client = anthropic.AsyncAnthropic(base_url=f"https://api.{environment}.acme.com/claude")
    sample = random.sample(golden, min(sample_size, len(golden)))

    passed = 0
    for case in sample:
        resp = await client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-5",  # uses the canary's pinned model
            max_tokens=512,
            system=case.get("system", "") + case.get("context", ""),
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": case["input"]}],
        )
        answer = "".join(b.text for b in resp.content if b.type == "text")
        verdict = await judge_answer(answer, case["expected_criteria"])
        if verdict:
            passed += 1

    pass_rate = passed / len(sample)
    return {"pass_rate": pass_rate, "sample_size": len(sample), "passed": passed}

async def main():
    with open("evals/golden.json") as f:
        golden = json.load(f)
    result = await sample_live_eval("canary", sample_size=50, golden=golden)
    min_pass_rate = 0.88
    if result["pass_rate"] < min_pass_rate:
        print(f"FAIL: post-soak eval {result['pass_rate']:.2%} < {min_pass_rate:.0%}")
        exit(1)
    print(f"PASS: post-soak eval {result['pass_rate']:.2%}")
Rollback Triggers and Automation
Every gate transition must have a corresponding rollback path. The rollback decision tree:
Canary auto-abort:
triggered automatically when a canary abort condition fires. Traffic reverts to the previous version within 60 seconds. On-call is paged.
Manual canary abort:
the on-call engineer or release engineer can trigger a manual abort via the release dashboard or a Slack command (
/rollback canary invoice-assistant
Post-launch rollback:
if a defect is discovered after full production rollout, the rollback procedure from the operational readiness runbook is invoked. Expected RTO < 15 minutes.

# Emergency rollback command (executed by on-call engineer)
# Reverts the prompt template to the prior version without a code deployment.

# 1. Find the last known-good prompt version tag
git tag -l 'prompts/invoice-assistant/*' | sort -V | tail -5
# Example output:
# prompts/invoice-assistant/v1.1.1
# prompts/invoice-assistant/v1.1.2
# prompts/invoice-assistant/v1.1.3   <-- current (broken)
# prompts/invoice-assistant/v1.2.0   <-- current (broken)

# 2. Update the registry to the prior tag
kubectl set env deployment/invoice-assistant   PROMPT_TEMPLATE_VERSION=prompts/invoice-assistant/v1.1.2

# 3. Restart pods to pick up the new registry value
kubectl rollout restart deployment/invoice-assistant

# 4. Verify rollback completed
kubectl rollout status deployment/invoice-assistant --timeout=120s

# 5. Confirm metrics are recovering
# Check Grafana: error rate should return to < 1% within 2-3 minutes
Promotion Gate Anti-Patterns
Several common shortcuts in promotion gate design undermine the protection the pipeline is supposed to provide:
Anti-pattern
How it manifests
Why it's dangerous
Correct approach
Skipping canary under time pressure
PM or EM approves bypassing the 30-minute soak to hit a deadline
A behaviour regression visible to 5% of users becomes visible to 100% of users simultaneously with no soak warning
Canary is never optional. The 30-minute minimum is the minimum, not a recommendation. If the deadline cannot accommodate it, delay the release.
Auto-promoting from staging without PM approval
Pipeline auto-promotes when staging gates pass, no human in the loop
A technically correct but product-incorrect change ships without the PM seeing it behave in staging
Staging-to-canary always requires an explicit human approval, not just gate passage.
Using staging eval results from a prior run
Release engineer reuses yesterday's staging eval result to avoid waiting for a new run
If the staging environment was updated after the eval ran, the results do not reflect the current state
Staging eval must be run against the specific SHA being promoted, within 24 hours of the promotion.
Canary abort thresholds set too loose
Error rate threshold set at 10% (vs the recommended 3×) to avoid 'false' aborts
A 5% error rate on 5% canary traffic is already a 5% error rate hitting users. A 10% threshold allows a significant regression to go undetected.
Set thresholds conservatively. A false abort is annoying but recoverable. A missed regression is an incident.
Rollback not tested before production
Team discovers at rollback time that the prior prompt version tag was never created
Rollback cannot be executed; RTO exceeds the SLA; incident extends
Test the rollback procedure in staging before every major version promotion.
Multi-Region Promotion
Progressive region rollout

For organisations running Claude integrations in multiple geographic regions, canary promotion can be staged across regions rather than across traffic percentages. This catches region-specific issues (latency from Anthropic's infrastructure, regulatory data residency problems) before full global rollout:

# Multi-region canary sequence
promotion_sequence:
  - region: us-west-2
    traffic_split: 1.0      # 100% in the first region
    soak_minutes: 15
    abort_on_error_rate: 0.03

  - region: eu-west-1
    traffic_split: 1.0
    soak_minutes: 15
    abort_on_error_rate: 0.03
    # EU may have different latency characteristics due to Anthropic API region
    latency_p95_threshold_ms: 6000  # higher SLA for EU

  - region: ap-southeast-1
    traffic_split: 1.0
    soak_minutes: 15
    abort_on_error_rate: 0.03

A failure in any region aborts the promotion and rolls back all regions, not just the failing one. Partial promotion (some regions on new version, others on old) creates inconsistent user experience and complicates debugging.
Feature Flags as a Promotion Layer
When feature flags complement promotion gates

Environment promotion gates control when an artifact reaches an environment. Feature flags control which users within that environment see the new behaviour. For Claude features, feature flags add a useful intermediate layer:
Internal preview:

deploy to production behind a feature flag enabled only for internal users. This exposes the feature to real production infrastructure (latency, actual API quotas, real user accounts) without any external users seeing it.
Gradual rollout beyond canary:
canary at 5% is the minimum; feature flags allow a finer progression — 1%, 5%, 20%, 50%, 100% — with a pause and evaluation at each step.
A/B testing:
run two prompt versions simultaneously (A=current, B=new) and compare their eval pass rates on live traffic. This is more robust than synthetic staging eval for detecting output distribution shifts.

# Feature flag check in the Claude integration
import os
from myapp.feature_flags import is_enabled

async def handle_invoice_query(user_id: str, account_id: str, message: str) -> Response:
    # Determine which prompt version to use based on feature flag
    if is_enabled("invoice-assistant-v2", user_id=user_id):
        template = registry.get("invoice-assistant", version="2.0.0")
    else:
        template = registry.get("invoice-assistant", version="1.2.3")

    # Both versions share the same code path; only the template differs
    response = await call_claude(
        template=template,
        context=build_context(account_id),
        user_message=message,
    )

    # Log which version was used for A/B analysis
    logger.info(
        "claude_api_call",
        feature="invoice-assistant",
        prompt_version=template.version,
        user_id_hash=sha256(user_id),
        flag_group="v2" if is_enabled("invoice-assistant-v2", user_id=user_id) else "control",
    )
    return response
Promotion Gate Metrics
Track these metrics to evaluate whether the promotion gate process is functioning correctly:
Canary abort rate:
fraction of canary deployments that are aborted before completing the soak period. Target < 5%. A rate above 10% indicates staging is not catching regressions before canary.
Promotion cycle time:
time from main branch merge to full production deployment (all gates passed). Target < 3 business days for standard changes. Above 5 days indicates gate bottlenecks.
Rollback rate:
fraction of full production deployments that require a rollback within 7 days. Target < 2%. Higher rates suggest the post-soak eval threshold is too lenient.
Approval wait time:
time between staging gates passing and human approval being granted. Track by change tier. Approval wait times above the SLA indicate approver availability problems.
Gate bypass rate:
fraction of deployments where any gate was explicitly bypassed (with justification). Target 0%. Any bypass is an exception and must be documented with a post-mortem note.
Summary

Environment promotion gates enforce that a Claude feature is verified at each stage before advancing to a wider audience. The four-gate pipeline — CI gates on PR merge, staging automated gates plus PM approval, 30-minute canary soak with auto-abort, and post-soak eval before 100% rollout — creates multiple independent verification points that catch different failure modes. The canary stage is particularly important for Claude features because live user traffic may cover input patterns not represented in the golden dataset.
Promotion to staging requires all CI gates green and registry consistency confirmed.
Promotion to canary requires staging automated gates (eval, load test) and human approval matching the change tier.
Canary soak is 30 minutes minimum at 5% traffic; auto-abort on error rate > 3×, latency > SLA, or cost > 2× staging.
Full production rollout requires a post-soak eval sample passing at ≥ 88%.
Every promotion gate has a corresponding rollback path with < 15-minute RTO.
